quote: You filled out a solar form online looking at solar panels for your home. Hello, Sean? No. I did.
stewart_read: Meg addresses the customer as 'Sean' mid-sentence — a name that does not match 'Robert,' the name she used at 00:03. Speaker_2 is present on the call, so the cause is uncertain: this may be a CRM data mismatch, a misread of the script, or a momentary address to the third party. What is clear is the effect: Robert hears a wrong name layered on top of a form he doesn't recognize, compounding the legitimacy gap before the pitch has started. The schema's protocols.how_did_you_get_my_number directive calls for honest specificity precisely because vagueness increases suspicion.
alternative_hypothesis: The 'Sean' fragment was directed at speaker_2 (the third party present), not at Robert — in which case it is not a name error but an unintended cross-talk that still damaged the opening.
coaching_implication: Manager should pull this moment in the 1-on-1 and ask Meg to walk through her pre-call CRM check. The coaching point is not 'don't say the wrong name' — it's 'confirm the name before you launch the intro, every time.' If the CRM had 'Sean' in the record, that's a data-hygiene flag to escalate. If it was cross-talk with speaker_2, the coaching point is to pause and re-anchor: 'Sorry — Robert, I want to make sure I'm talking to the right person.'

quote: I gotcha. Or go to Florida.
stewart_read: Speaker_2 interjects here with a comment that tracks the conversation ('I gotcha. Or go to Florida.') — this is an engaged third party, not background noise. Per schema protocols.spouse_decision and protocols.adult_child_decision_maker, any co-present party who engages in the conversation is a potential co-decision-maker signal. Meg never acknowledges speaker_2, never asks who they are, and never checks whether they are on the title or involved in the decision. The call proceeds as if only Robert is present.
alternative_hypothesis: Speaker_2 could be a visitor or non-decision-maker with no title interest — in which case the omission is lower-stakes. But the schema's protocol is to check, not to assume.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this moment to reinforce the protocols.spouse_decision check: when a third party speaks on the call, the rep's job is to identify them. A simple 'Oh — is someone else there with you?' takes three seconds and either surfaces a co-decision-maker or rules one out. Skipping it is a structural vulnerability on any call that reaches appointment stage.

quote: Oh, no. Maybe this summer if I get the right price on my end.
stewart_read: Robert's move is conditional — 'if I get the right price.' This is not a hard disqualifier; it is a soft, future-state objection. The schema's objection_inversion framework is directly applicable here: the flip available is something like 'Some sellers find it worth knowing what a design looks like before they list — want to see what it would show?' This creates curiosity without making a factual claim about resale outcomes. Meg instead treats the conditional as a hard DQ and self-terminates. Strategic read: there is no evidence Meg was setting up a later move — she exits immediately and offers no callback or future hook.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg recognized a near-certain no-show risk and cut the call to protect her sit rate. This is plausible given the comp model (paid on sits, not bookings), but there is no evidence she attempted even a low-cost future-state hook before exiting.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk Meg through the objection_inversion framework on this specific moment. The coaching question is: 'What's the cost of a 30-second curiosity hook before you take someone off the list?' The suggested rephrase — 'Some sellers find it worth knowing what a design looks like before they list — want to see what it would show?' — does not assert a market outcome; it creates a reason to stay on the call. Flag that this leverage point would land harder if Meg had actual resale data from Ion's market, per the schema's numeric_discipline flag protocol.

quote: You did any houses near me in the area?
stewart_read: Robert asks unprompted whether Ion has done work near him — this is a social-proof inquiry, not a stall. It signals residual curiosity about solar even after the move objection. He then volunteers his neighbor Laurie's name at 01:44. This is the highest-engagement moment in the call. Meg's response ('Am I able to look them up in my system? Alright. Understood.') is a non-answer that extinguishes the signal entirely. A rep who recognized this as an enthusiasm signal would have used it as a re-entry point: 'I can't pull up individual accounts, but if Laurie's your neighbor, she's probably seen the same kind of savings you would — that's actually why I wanted to talk to you.'
coaching_implication: Manager should highlight this as the call's missed pivot point. The Laurie reference is a social-proof anchor that arrived organically — exactly the kind of moment the schema's verify.interest_in_solar depth-grading is designed to catch. Meg had already decided to exit and couldn't see it. The coaching question: 'When a customer asks about their neighbor's system, what does that tell you about their interest level?' Use this to build the habit of treating social-proof questions as re-entry signals, not conversation-enders.

quote: Yeah. No. It totally gets you there. Well, what I could do, I'll go ahead and, I'll take you off our list. If you end up staying and you wanna look at solar options, just give me a call and let me know.
stewart_read: This is the call's defining moment. Meg self-terminates on a conditional objection ('maybe this summer if I get the right price') with no reframe, no curiosity hook, and no future-state offer. 'Give me a call' is not a callback — it places all forward motion on the customer. The schema's objection_inversion framework and the analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction directive both flag this: a callback with a locked time is materially different from 'call me if things change.' Meg leaves no door open that she controls.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg is protecting her sit rate by not booking a likely no-show. This is the most defensible version of the exit. But even under this read, the correct move is a future-state hook ('Let me put a note to follow up in 60 days — if the house hasn't sold, we'll take a look then'), not a passive 'give me a call.'
coaching_implication: Manager should use this as the primary coaching beat. The question for Meg: 'What's the difference between taking someone off the list and putting them on a future-touch sequence?' The coaching goal is to build the habit of owning the next contact, even on soft exits. A suggested rephrase: 'I'll make a note to reach back out in a couple months — if the house is still yours, we'll take a look then. Sound fair?' This costs nothing and keeps the lead alive.

quote: Weird. Okay. It looks like we got some information two days ago, for the 2 Graham Road home. Have you ever looked at solar, or do you know much about the program?
stewart_read: After Robert denies filling out the form, Meg's 'Weird. Okay.' is an acknowledgment without resolution. She pivots to a generic interest question rather than closing the legitimacy gap. The schema's protocols.how_did_you_get_my_number directive calls for honest specificity — 'Sometimes these come through a partner site' or a similar bridge would have addressed Robert's implicit 'why are you calling me?' Meg instead moves forward with the gap open, which makes every subsequent move harder.
coaching_implication: Manager should pair this with the 00:15 moment. The two-part coaching point: (1) confirm the name before launching the intro; (2) when the customer denies the form, close the legitimacy gap before moving forward. A suggested rephrase: 'Sometimes these come through a partner site — either way, you're on our list as someone who was looking. Have you thought about solar at all?' This doesn't require Meg to know exactly how the lead was generated; it just bridges the gap without leaving it open.

quote: Am I able to look them up in my system? Alright. Understood. You know?
stewart_read: Robert asks a direct social-proof question about his neighbor Laurie. Meg's response is a half-formed self-question ('Am I able to look them up in my system?') followed by an immediate retreat ('Alright. Understood.'). This is a knowledge-gap moment handled by dodge-and-retreat rather than by transparent acknowledgment and bridge. The schema's analysis_directives.knowledge_gap_handling directive distinguishes between dodge, admit-and-recover, and fabricate — Meg's response is closest to dodge, which forfeits the social-proof opportunity entirely.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this to build the admit-and-recover habit. The coaching point: 'You don't need to look up Laurie to use her as a bridge.' A suggested rephrase: 'I can't pull up individual accounts, but if she's your neighbor, she's probably seen the same kind of savings you would — that's actually why I wanted to talk to you.' This turns the knowledge gap into a social-proof anchor rather than a dead end.

quote: Yeah. Well, do you have plans to move, like, in the next year or so, or is this more pushed out?
stewart_read: Meg's follow-up question on the move timeline is the one moment of genuine qualification work in the call. She correctly identifies that the move timeline matters and probes it directly. This is a competent move — she's checking whether the objection is hard or soft. The problem is what she does with the answer: when Robert says 'maybe this summer,' she treats it as a hard DQ rather than a conditional that warrants a reframe. The qualification instinct is right; the exit decision is wrong.
coaching_implication: Manager should acknowledge this as the one thing Meg did well — she probed the timeline rather than accepting the objection at face value. The coaching point is the next step: 'You asked the right question. What do you do when the answer is conditional?' Build from the competent move to the missing reframe.
